walkthrough.title: Choose the Ghost Player
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get Mysterium Park set up. First, your group needs to decide who will play as the ghost. The ghost is not a psychic like everyone else. Instead, the ghost knows the truth about what happened but cannot speak, gesture, or communicate in any way except by giving vision cards. Everyone else will be psychics working together to solve the mystery. If this is your first game, pick someone who is comfortable being creative and quiet at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Place the fairground game board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. This circular board is the heart of the park. It has a ticket track around the edge that you will use to count the six rounds of play. Place the ticket marker on the first space of the track.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Character Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the character cards. These are the suspects in the mystery. Lay them out in a row or a circle near the board so every psychic can see them clearly. Each character is a colorful figure from the amusement park. For your first game, use the number of characters recommended for your player count in the rulebook. More characters means a harder game.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Location Cards
walkthrough.text: Next, find the location cards. These represent places in the park where the crime could have happened. Lay them out in a similar fashion near the character cards so psychics can see all of them at once. Just like characters, the number of locations scales with player count.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Ghost Screen
walkthrough.text: The ghost player now takes the ghost screen and sets it up in front of them. This screen hides the ghost's secret information from the psychics. Behind the screen, the ghost will manage their hand of vision cards and the plot cards that reveal the truth. No peeking allowed.

walkthrough.title: Assign the Plot Cards
walkthrough.text: Behind the screen, the ghost draws plot cards that secretly assign one character and one location to each psychic player. The ghost looks at these assignments and memorizes or keeps them organized. Each psychic has a unique character and a unique location that they need to identify, but they do not know what these are yet. This is the mystery the team needs to solve.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Vision Cards
walkthrough.text: The ghost takes the entire deck of vision cards and shuffles them thoroughly. These are the large, beautifully illustrated cards with abstract, dreamlike artwork. The ghost draws a hand of seven vision cards and keeps them hidden behind the screen. The rest of the deck stays within the ghost's reach for drawing later. Vision cards are the ghost's only tool for communicating with the psychics.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Psychic Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each psychic player takes a psychic token in their chosen color and an innocence token marker. These tokens will be used to mark your guesses on the character and location cards during the game. Place them in front of you so they are ready for the first round. The game is now set up and ready to play.

bullets: One player is the ghost — knows the truth but cannot speak or gesture
All other players are psychics working cooperatively
Ghost communicates only through vision cards

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Mysterium Park is played in two phases over a maximum of six rounds. In Phase one, each psychic must identify their assigned character. In Phase two, each psychic must identify their assigned location. After both phases, the team faces a final revelation where they must agree on the true culprit. Everyone wins or loses together, so cooperation is everything.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Each round follows the same pattern. First, the ghost gives vision cards as clues. Then the psychics discuss what they see. Next, each psychic makes a guess. Finally, the ghost reveals whether each guess was correct or incorrect. After the reveals, the ticket marker advances and the ghost draws back up to seven vision cards.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Gives Clues
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, the ghost selects one or more vision cards from their hand and places them face up in front of each psychic who still needs a clue. These cards are abstract and dreamlike with no text. The ghost must creatively choose cards whose colors, objects, themes, or moods connect to the psychic's assigned character or location. A card showing fire might hint at a fire-breather character. A card with water might point toward the fountain location.

walkthrough.title: Psychics Discuss
walkthrough.text: After receiving their vision cards, all psychics discuss openly. Look at the artwork carefully and try to find connections between the vision card and the available characters or locations. Compare what other psychics received too, because seeing all the clues together can help narrow things down. Collaboration is not just encouraged, it is essential. Fresh eyes often spot connections that the intended psychic misses.

walkthrough.title: Psychics Guess
walkthrough.text: Once discussion is done, each psychic places their token on the character or location they believe matches their clue. You do not have to agree with other psychics about your own guess, but listening to their input is almost always a good idea. Everyone guesses simultaneously, so there is no advantage to going first or last.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Reveals Results
walkthrough.text: The ghost now indicates whether each psychic guessed correctly or incorrectly. If a psychic guessed right, that character or location is eliminated from the pool and marked with an innocence token. That psychic is done with this phase and waits for the others. If a psychic guessed wrong, they will try again next round with new vision cards. Each round with at least one wrong guess costs the team a ticket on the track.

walkthrough.title: Why Elimination Matters
walkthrough.text: Every correct guess removes a character or location from the pool. This is incredibly important because it narrows the options for everyone else. If there are eight characters and three have been eliminated, the remaining psychics only have five to choose from. Pay close attention to what gets eliminated. It makes every future guess easier and is the key to winning before time runs out.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two: Locations
walkthrough.text: Once all psychics have correctly identified their characters, Phase two begins. The process is identical but now the ghost gives vision cards pointing toward locations instead. All the same rules apply. Psychics discuss, guess, and the ghost reveals. Correct guesses eliminate locations from the pool. The six-round timer continues from wherever it left off in Phase one, so speed in Phase one gives you more time for Phase two.

walkthrough.title: The Ghost's Hand Management
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about the ghost's options. After each round, the ghost draws back up to seven vision cards. If the ghost's hand has nothing useful, they may discard their entire hand and draw seven new cards, but this costs one ticket on the track. It is the same as wasting a round. Use this option sparingly and only when your hand truly has no viable connections to give.

walkthrough.title: The Final Revelation
walkthrough.text: After both phases are complete, the remaining non-eliminated characters and locations form the suspect pairs for the final revelation. The ghost gives one last set of vision cards as a final clue. All psychics must discuss and unanimously agree on which remaining character-location pair is the true culprit. This is the most critical moment of the game.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: If the psychics correctly identify the true culprit pair in the final revelation, everyone wins. If they choose wrong, everyone loses. The team can also lose if all six tickets are used before completing both phases, depending on which variant you are playing. There is no numeric score. Mysterium Park is purely win or lose, which makes every guess feel high-stakes.

walkthrough.title: The Communication Rule
walkthrough.text: One final but crucial rule. The ghost cannot talk, nod, shake their head, point, make facial expressions, or communicate in any way other than placing vision cards. This is the hardest part of being the ghost. Even a raised eyebrow can give away information. Psychics should avoid watching the ghost's reactions during discussion. The ghost's only voice is the artwork on the vision cards.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: 2 phases: Phase 1 = identify characters, Phase 2 = identify locations
Max 6 rounds total across both phases
Final revelation after both phases → win or lose as a team

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Park
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Mysterium Park. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted scenario with one ghost and three psychics. Even if your group is a different size, follow along to see how each phase works and learn from common beginner mistakes. Let's solve a mystery together.

walkthrough.title: The Setup So Far
walkthrough.text: The ghost has set up behind the screen. Six characters are laid out for all to see, and six locations are arranged nearby. The ghost has secretly assigned one character and one location to each of the three psychics. The ghost has drawn seven vision cards and is studying their hand. Phase one begins now with character identification.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Ghost Gives Clues
walkthrough.text: The ghost looks at their seven vision cards and needs to find connections to each psychic's assigned character. For Psychic one, the target character is a fire-breather. The ghost finds a vision card showing a sunset with warm orange and red tones, and places it face up in front of Psychic one. For Psychic two, the ghost places a card with a dark forest scene. For Psychic three, a card showing a carnival tent with stripes.

walkthrough.title: The Psychics Discuss
walkthrough.text: Now the fun begins. Psychic one looks at the sunset card and says it feels fiery and warm. The group scans the six characters and notices the fire-breather has red and orange in their outfit. Psychic two has a dark forest card and suggests it could mean the fortune teller, who has a mysterious vibe. The group also considers the magician, who wears a dark cape. Psychic three's carnival tent card could match several characters. The group talks it through together.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Guesses and Results
walkthrough.text: Psychic one places their token on the fire-breather. Correct! The fire-breather is eliminated from the suspect pool and Psychic one moves on to wait for Phase two. Psychic two chooses the magician instead of the fortune teller. Incorrect. They will get a new clue next round. Psychic three guesses the strongman based on the tent. Also incorrect. The ticket marker advances one space.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ignoring Color Connections
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what Psychic two missed. The dark forest vision card had deep purples and mysterious shadows. The fortune teller's card also has purple tones and a mystical atmosphere. Psychic two fixated on the dark cape of the magician and missed the color match. In Mysterium Park, color is often the ghost's strongest tool. Always check if the dominant colors in a vision card match any character's outfit or background before looking for thematic connections.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: New Clues
walkthrough.text: The ghost draws back up to seven cards and gives new vision cards to Psychic two and Psychic three. Psychic two receives a card with crystal balls and starry patterns. This time the group immediately connects the mystical imagery to the fortune teller. Psychic three gets a card showing heavy chains and metal. The strongman uses chains in his act, but so does the escapist character.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Not Using Elimination
walkthrough.text: Psychic three is torn between the strongman and the escapist. But wait. The fire-breather was already eliminated in Round one. That means there are only five characters left, not six. Psychic three forgot to factor in the elimination. New players often keep considering eliminated options, which wastes mental energy and leads to confusion. Always check the innocence tokens first to see which characters are already cleared before narrowing your guess.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Results
walkthrough.text: Psychic two guesses the fortune teller this time. Correct! Another character is eliminated. Psychic three decides on the escapist because the chains feel more like escape acts than weightlifting. Correct! All three psychics have identified their characters. Phase one is complete, and we have used only two of our six rounds. That leaves four rounds for Phase two and the final revelation.

walkthrough.title: Phase Two Begins: Locations
walkthrough.text: Now the ghost must guide each psychic to their assigned location. The process is identical. The ghost gives vision cards, psychics discuss and guess, and the ghost reveals. Three characters have been eliminated, meaning three locations will also be identified and eliminated. The remaining non-eliminated characters and locations will form the suspect pairs for the final revelation.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Ghost Being Too Clever
walkthrough.text: The ghost has a vision card showing a bird perched on a branch. Psychic one's target location is the roller coaster. The ghost thinks the swooping feeling of a bird diving is like a roller coaster drop. This is way too abstract. The psychics stare at the bird card and start talking about the aviary, the garden, and the forest tent. Nobody mentions the roller coaster. When you are the ghost, choose the most obvious, direct connection available. Cleverness is the enemy. If you have to explain the connection in your head with more than one logical step, it is probably too subtle.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Recovers with a Better Card
walkthrough.text: The psychics guess wrong on that round. Next round, the ghost finds a vision card with steep hills and a winding path. This time the group immediately connects the winding path to the roller coaster track. Psychic one guesses correctly. The lesson is clear. One strong, obvious card beats two clever ones. The ghost should always look for the simplest visual match in their hand.

walkthrough.title: Mistake: Psychics Not Helping Each Other
walkthrough.text: In Round four, Psychic three receives a vision card and stares at it alone for a long time. Nobody else chimes in. Psychic three eventually picks the wrong location. Here is the problem: Mysterium Park is fully cooperative. Every psychic should be studying every vision card, not just their own. Other players often see connections you miss because they bring a different perspective. If your table goes quiet, that is a red flag. Talk more.

walkthrough.title: Completing Phase Two
walkthrough.text: After some more discussion-heavy rounds, all three psychics identify their locations. Phase two is complete. We have used five of our six rounds total. Three characters and three locations have been eliminated, leaving three characters and three locations as possible culprit pairs. Now comes the final revelation.

walkthrough.title: The Final Revelation
walkthrough.text: The ghost places one final set of vision cards on the table as a last clue. The psychics must now agree on which remaining character-location pair is the true culprit. The group discusses intensely. One card has amusement park lights that remind them of the carousel, and a shadowy figure that matches the illusionist. The group agrees on the illusionist at the carousel. The ghost reveals the answer, and they are correct. The team wins!

walkthrough.title: Lessons Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's recap the key mistakes from this practice game. First, always check color connections before thematic ones. Second, track eliminations carefully and do not waste time on cleared suspects. Third, as the ghost, choose the most obvious visual match, not the cleverest. Fourth, all psychics should discuss every clue, not just their own. These four habits will dramatically improve your win rate.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your own game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy advice for both the ghost and the psychics.

Second, reference the Appendix for quick definitions of game terms and mechanics.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun solving the mystery!

summary.title: Before You Play
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy for ghost and psychics
Reference the Appendix → quick definitions of game terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Ghost Tip: Prioritize Color Matching
walkthrough.text: As the ghost, your strongest tool is color. Before looking for thematic connections, scan your vision cards for dominant colors that match a character's outfit or a location's palette. If a character wears bright red and you have a vision card drenched in red tones, that card will almost always be understood. Color is universal and does not require the psychics to think the same way you do. Save thematic connections for when color is not an option.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Tip: Less Is More
walkthrough.text: New ghosts often give two or three vision cards per psychic, thinking more clues means a better chance of guessing correctly. The opposite is often true. Multiple cards can confuse psychics because they try to find a single character that matches all the cards, and that intersection might not exist in their minds. One well-chosen card with a clear color or object match is far better than three vague ones. Only give multiple cards when each one independently points to the same target.

walkthrough.title: Psychic Tip: Study All the Clues
walkthrough.text: Do not just focus on your own vision card. Look at what every other psychic received. If Psychic one's card clearly points to the fortune teller, and you were also considering the fortune teller for your own clue, you can safely rule them out. Cross-referencing clues across the table is one of the most powerful strategies in the game. The ghost gave different cards to different psychics for a reason.

walkthrough.title: Psychic Tip: Trust Your First Instinct
walkthrough.text: When you first look at a vision card, your gut reaction is usually the one the ghost intended. Mysterium Park rewards intuitive leaps more than analytical breakdowns. If a card makes you immediately think of the juggler, that is probably the right answer. Overthinking leads to second-guessing, which leads to wrong answers. The ghost chose the most obvious connection available to them, so look for the most obvious match you can see.

walkthrough.title: Both: Track Eliminations Carefully
walkthrough.text: Every time a character or location is eliminated, it makes the remaining guesses easier. But only if you actually pay attention. Keep the innocence tokens visible and remind the table which options are gone at the start of each round. Some groups like to physically remove eliminated cards from the display. However you do it, make elimination tracking a habit. It is free information that makes every guess more accurate.

walkthrough.title: Ghost Tip: Plan Multiple Rounds Ahead
walkthrough.text: Do not just look at this round's clues in isolation. When you draw your hand of seven vision cards, mentally map which cards work for which psychics and which ones are duds. If you have two great cards for Psychic one but nothing for Psychic two, consider holding one of those cards for next round and giving Psychic two the best available option now. You have six rounds maximum, so pacing your best cards across rounds is better than frontloading everything and being stuck with a bad hand later.

walkthrough.title: Psychic Tip: Speak Up About What You See
walkthrough.text: Even if you are not the psychic who received the clue, say what you see in the vision card. Maybe you notice a tiny umbrella in the background that reminds you of the carousel. Maybe the lighting feels like a stage spotlight. Every observation is valuable in a cooperative game. The psychics who talk the most and share the most observations tend to win more games. There is no penalty for being wrong in discussion, only for being quiet.

walkthrough.title: Both: The Final Revelation Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Every correct guess during Phases one and two eliminates suspects and makes the final revelation easier. But even a perfect run can be lost if you rush the final vote. When the final vision cards come out, take your time. Discuss every remaining pair thoroughly. Look for connections between the final cards and each remaining option. A two-minute discussion at the end is worth more than saving thirty seconds. The final revelation is the only thing that determines whether you win or lose.

summary.title: Ghost Tip: Prioritize Color Matching
bullets: Color is the ghost's strongest tool — scan for palette matches first
Color is universal; thematic links require shared thinking
Save thematic connections for when color isn't available

summary.title: Ghost Tip: Less Is More
bullets: 1 well-chosen card beats 2-3 vague ones
Multiple cards can confuse — psychics try to match ALL cards
Only give multiple cards when each independently points to the target

summary.title: Psychic Tip: Study All the Clues
bullets: Look at every psychic's vision cards, not just your own
Cross-reference to eliminate candidates from your own guess
Ghost gave different cards for a reason — use that info

summary.title: Psychic Tip: Trust Your First Instinct
bullets: Gut reaction is usually what the ghost intended
Overthinking leads to second-guessing and wrong answers
Ghost chose the most obvious connection — look for it

summary.title: Both: Track Eliminations Carefully
bullets: Keep innocence tokens visible — remind table at start of each round
Some groups physically remove eliminated cards for clarity
Free information that makes every future guess more accurate

summary.title: Ghost Tip: Plan Multiple Rounds Ahead
bullets: Map which cards work for which psychics when you draw
Pace your best cards — don't frontload everything
Hold a strong card for next round if this round is covered

summary.title: Psychic Tip: Speak Up About What You See
bullets: Share observations on ALL clues, not just your own
Tiny details (background objects, lighting, mood) matter
No penalty for wrong observations — only for staying quiet

summary.title: Both: The Final Revelation Is Everything
bullets: All earlier phases exist to narrow down the final vote
Take time discussing every remaining pair thoroughly
A 2-minute final discussion is worth more than saving 30 seconds

walkthrough.title: Ghost: Build a Visual Language
walkthrough.text: Experienced ghost players develop a visual language with their group over multiple games. If you consistently use color as your primary signal, your psychics will learn to look for color first. If you tend to focus on objects in the foreground, they will scan foreground elements. Consistency builds trust and efficiency. After a few games together, your group will develop a shared visual vocabulary that makes communication through abstract art much smoother.

walkthrough.title: Ghost: Use Negative Space
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best card is not the one that matches your target but the one that does not match anything else. If five of the six remaining characters are outdoorsy and your target is the only indoor character, a vision card showing an indoor scene eliminates five options even if the specific room does not match. This is called using negative space. Give a card that could only point to one character by process of elimination rather than by direct match.

walkthrough.title: Ghost: Strategic Hand Cycling
walkthrough.text: The decision to discard your entire hand and redraw is one of the ghost's most important choices. Do not waste this on Round one when you have plenty of time. The best time to cycle your hand is when two or more psychics need clues and your hand has nothing for any of them. Cycling on Round three or four when you are running low on tickets is almost never worth it. Save your redraws for the early-to-middle game when you have time to spare and multiple psychics to serve.

walkthrough.title: Psychic: Process of Elimination
walkthrough.text: Advanced psychics do not just look for what a vision card matches. They look for what it does not match. If a card is full of bright yellows and greens, it probably does not point to the dark-themed fortune teller or the shadow-draped magician. By ruling out options the card clearly does not match, you can narrow the field before even considering positive matches. Combine this with tracking eliminations from previous rounds and your guess becomes much more precise.

walkthrough.title: Psychic: Read the Ghost's Patterns
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to how your ghost gives clues across rounds. If the ghost gave one card to Psychic one and two cards to Psychic two, it might mean Psychic one's clue is strong and Psychic two's is weak. If the ghost seems to favor color matches, lean into color interpretation. If a previous round's clue was clearly object-based, expect more of the same. The ghost is a person with consistent tendencies, and reading those tendencies is a meta-skill that improves with every game you play together.

walkthrough.title: Team: Optimize Phase One Speed
walkthrough.text: Every round saved in Phase one is a round gained in Phase two and the final revelation. Experienced teams prioritize getting Phase one done in two or three rounds at most. This means psychics should be willing to make confident guesses early rather than playing it safe. A wrong guess costs one round, but excessive caution also costs rounds. If you are seventy percent confident, guess. The elimination from a correct guess helps every other psychic more than one extra round of deliberation.

walkthrough.title: Team: Final Revelation Preparation
walkthrough.text: Smart teams start preparing for the final revelation during Phase two. As locations get eliminated, mentally note which character-location pairs remain. Discuss with the group what kind of vision card would distinguish between the remaining pairs. When the ghost gives the final clue, you will already have a framework for interpreting it. Teams that wait until the final revelation to start thinking about the remaining pairs are at a significant disadvantage compared to those who have been tracking the narrowing pool all along.

walkthrough.title: Scaling Difficulty
walkthrough.text: Once your group is winning consistently, there are several ways to increase the challenge. Add more characters and locations to the pool so there are more options to narrow down. The ghost can give fewer vision cards per psychic, forcing clearer single-card communication. You can also impose a time limit on discussion to add pressure. For the ultimate challenge, try playing with only two psychics, which means fewer eliminations and a harder final revelation. Adjusting difficulty keeps the game fresh as your group improves.

summary.title: Ghost: Build a Visual Language
bullets: Use consistent clue types (color, objects, mood) across games
Psychics learn to read your style over multiple sessions
Consistency > creativity for reliable communication

summary.title: Ghost: Use Negative Space
bullets: Give a card that could only match one target by process of elimination
If 5 suspects are outdoorsy and target is indoor → use indoor card
Exclusion-based clues can be stronger than direct matches

summary.title: Ghost: Strategic Hand Cycling
bullets: Best time to cycle: early-mid game with multiple psychics needing clues
Don't waste on Round 1 — you have time
Don't cycle on Rounds 4-5 — tickets are too precious

summary.title: Psychic: Process of Elimination
bullets: Look for what the vision card does NOT match first
Rule out options by color/mood mismatch
Combine with elimination tracking for precise guesses

summary.title: Psychic: Read the Ghost's Patterns
bullets: Track whether ghost favors color, objects, or themes
Number of cards given can indicate confidence level
Reading ghost tendencies improves with repeated play

summary.title: Team: Optimize Phase One Speed
bullets: Every round saved in Phase 1 = extra time for Phase 2
Guess at 70%+ confidence — wrong guesses cost 1 round, hesitation also costs rounds
Early correct guesses help all remaining psychics via elimination

summary.title: Team: Final Revelation Preparation
bullets: Track remaining suspect pairs during Phase 2, not after
Discuss what kind of clue would distinguish remaining pairs
Pre-built framework makes final vision cards easier to interpret

summary.title: Scaling Difficulty
bullets: Add more characters/locations for a harder pool
Ghost gives fewer cards per psychic for tighter clues
Add discussion time limits for pressure
2-player (1 ghost + 1 psychic) = ultimate challenge

entries.definition: Large, beautifully illustrated cards with abstract, dreamlike artwork. No text — purely visual. The ghost's only tool for communicating with psychics. There are 84 vision cards in the game.
Cards depicting suspects in the mystery. There are 13 character cards total. A subset is used each game based on player count. Characters are eliminated when correctly identified by psychics.
Cards depicting places in the amusement park where the crime could have occurred. There are 12 location cards total. A subset is used each game based on player count.
A foldable screen the ghost player uses to hide their plot cards, vision card hand, and secret assignments from the psychics. All ghost information stays behind this screen.
Secret cards drawn by the ghost during setup that assign one character and one location to each psychic player. These define the mystery the team must solve.
A track on the game board that counts the rounds. The game has a maximum of six rounds across both phases. The ticket marker advances when at least one psychic guesses incorrectly in a round.
Markers placed on character or location cards when they are correctly identified and eliminated from the suspect pool. They indicate which options are no longer in play.
Colored tokens used by psychic players to mark their guesses on character or location cards during each round.
One player who knows the truth about the mystery. The ghost cannot speak, gesture, or communicate except by placing vision cards. The ghost assigns secret character-location pairs to each psychic.
All players other than the ghost. Psychics work cooperatively to interpret vision card clues and identify their assigned characters and locations. Psychics may talk freely among themselves.
The first phase of the game where each psychic must correctly identify their assigned character. The ghost gives vision cards as clues each round. Correct guesses eliminate characters from the pool.
The second phase where each psychic must identify their assigned location. Same process as Phase One but for locations. The six-round timer continues from Phase One.

The climactic end of the game. Non-eliminated characters and locations form suspect pairs. The ghost gives one final set of vision cards. Psychics must unanimously agree on the true culprit pair. Correct = win, incorrect = lose.
When a psychic correctly identifies their character or location, it is removed from the suspect pool and marked with an innocence token. Elimination narrows future options and is key to winning.

The ghost maintains a hand of seven vision cards. After each round, the ghost draws back up to seven. The ghost may also discard their entire hand and redraw, but this costs one ticket on the round track.

The ghost cannot talk, nod, shake their head, point, make facial expressions, or communicate in any way other than placing vision cards. Psychics should avoid watching the ghost's reactions during discussion.

A strategy where the ghost selects vision cards whose dominant colors match the target character's outfit or location's palette. Color is generally the most reliable and universally understood clue type.

An advanced ghost strategy where you give a card that does not match most remaining options rather than one that directly matches the target. Works when the target is visually distinct from the rest of the pool.

A psychic strategy where you study all vision cards given to all psychics, not just your own. If another psychic's card clearly points to a character you were considering, you can rule it out for yourself.

When the ghost discards their entire hand of vision cards and draws seven new ones. Costs one ticket on the round track. Best used early in the game when multiple psychics need clues and the current hand is unusable.

The number of characters and locations used varies by player count. Fewer players means fewer eliminations during phases, which makes the final revelation harder. Two players (one ghost, one psychic) is the most challenging configuration.
Groups can increase difficulty by adding more characters and locations to the pool, limiting the ghost to fewer vision cards per clue, or imposing time limits on psychic discussion.
Mysterium Park is fully cooperative — all players win or lose together. There is no competitive scoring. The team either correctly identifies the true culprit pair or they do not.

A combination of one character and one location that could be the true culprit. After eliminations, the remaining non-eliminated characters and locations form the suspect pairs for the final revelation.

Vision cards feature surreal, dreamlike artwork with no text. They can be interpreted in many ways depending on the viewer. This ambiguity is the core challenge of the game — the ghost must pick art that evokes a specific target.
